---
title: "A Self-Updating Montreal Events Guide"
description: "Turning a ChatGPT-maintained Google Doc into a live events page with a Claude Code skill, a JSON schema, and zero hosting or API costs."
date: "2026-07-16"
image: thumbnail.png
draft: true
categories: [claude, automation, quarto]
---

I keep a curated Google Doc of Montréal events — festivals, museum exhibits, trivia nights, hikes — that ChatGPT refreshes weekly.
It's genuinely useful, but it's a document: you open it, scroll, and lose your place. I
wanted it as a page — filterable, sorted, with a "what's on this weekend" view — without
signing up for a new API key or standing up a service that costs money to run. This is
how I got there, and where the plan didn't survive contact with reality.

## The doc-as-API trick

The Doc is link-shared, and link-shared Google Docs expose an unauthenticated export
endpoint: `.../export?format=txt`. No OAuth, no service account, no API key — just a
plain-text dump of the doc's contents, reachable from anywhere. `fetch_doc.py` hits that
URL and hands the raw text to the extraction step. It's not a documented API and it could
change, but for a personal project it's the entire integration cost of "get the doc's
contents into Python": one GET request.

## LLM extraction behind a schema validator

The doc isn't structured data — it's prose written for a human, formatted however
ChatGPT felt like formatting it that week. A regex or hand-rolled parser would be
chasing the doc's layout forever. So extraction is an LLM step: read the doc text,
emit `events.json`. The part that makes that safe is that the LLM never gets to publish
directly — everything it produces has to pass a schema validator first.

That's `montreal_events/events.schema.json`, a JSON Schema for the whole file:

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["last_updated", "source_doc", "events"],
  "additionalProperties": false,
  "properties": {
    "last_updated": { "type": "string", "pattern": "^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$" },
    "source_doc": { "type": "string", "minLength": 1 },
    "events": { "type": "array", "items": { "$ref": "#/$defs/event" } }
  }
}
```

and `montreal_events/validate.py` is the gate that runs it, plus a handful of checks a
schema alone can't express — no duplicate ids, `start_date` before `end_date`, nothing
already expired:

```python
def validate_events(data: dict, today: datetime.date) -> list[str]:
    schema = json.loads(SCHEMA_PATH.read_text())
    errors = [
        f"schema: {e.json_path}: {e.message}"
        for e in Draft202012Validator(schema).iter_errors(data)
    ]
    if errors:
        return errors  # semantic checks assume structural validity

    if data["last_updated"] != today.isoformat():
        errors.append(
            f"last_updated is {data['last_updated']}, expected {today.isoformat()}"
        )

    seen = set()
    for ev in data["events"]:
        eid = ev["id"]
        if eid in seen:
            errors.append(f"duplicate id: {eid}")
        seen.add(eid)

        start = _parse_date(ev["start_date"], "start_date", eid, errors)
        end = _parse_date(ev["end_date"], "end_date", eid, errors)
        if start and end and start > end:
            errors.append(f"{eid}: start_date after end_date")
        if ev["status"] == "date-specific" and end and end < today:
            errors.append(f"{eid}: expired (ended {end.isoformat()})")

    return errors
```

If `events.json` doesn't pass, nothing gets committed. The LLM can be as sloppy as it
wants with formatting; it can't get a malformed or duplicate event past the gate.

That bet paid off faster than I expected. One week in, the doc's author restructured it
completely — entry cards with canonical ids (`EVT-003`, `MUS-007`...), pipe-delimited
fields, a calendar index, archive and wishlist sections. A deterministic parser tuned to
the old layout would have needed a rewrite. Instead, the fix was entirely prompt rules —
a few paragraphs of updated extraction instructions in the skill's `SKILL.md` describing
the new card format. Zero lines of Python changed. The doc's own ids even became our
event ids and calendar-sync keys, which cost nothing to adopt since the calendar map was
still empty at that point.

## In-session skill vs. paid CI

I considered a GitHub Action on a schedule: pull the doc, call an LLM API, validate,
commit, done, fully hands-off. I didn't build it. That path means an API key sitting in
repo secrets and a per-run cost for the extraction call, for a doc that changes at most
weekly. Instead this runs as a Claude Code skill (`/update-montreal-events`) that I invoke
in-session when I want a refresh: fetch the doc, extract, validate, check links, commit.
Same steps, but it rides on a Claude Code session I'm already paying for instead of a
metered API key living in CI. The trade is that it's not automatic — someone has to run
it — which is a fine trade for something that updates on a weekly cadence anyway.

## Calendar sync via MCP

The other half of "useful" is having these events show up somewhere I already look: a
shared Google Calendar. That's driven by the Google Calendar MCP connector, with
`montreal_events/calendar_map.json` doing the dedup — a flat `id -> gcal_event_id`
mapping so re-running the sync is a no-op instead of a pile of duplicate calendar entries.

The one wrinkle was authorization, not code: the group calendar lives on a different
Google account than the one connected to Claude. The fix was on the calendar side, not
ours — share the calendar to the connector's account with "Make changes to events," and
the MCP then has full access by calendar id. No design change needed.

The first real sync created 27 all-day events (11 festivals/events, 14 exhibits, 2
Ottawa day trips) and populated `calendar_map.json` with all 27 id mappings. The one
convention I had to invent along the way was for open-ended exhibits: a doc entry like
"until X" has no real start date, and putting a month-long bar on the calendar for every
one of those would wallpaper it. Instead those become a single-day "closing marker" — an
all-day event titled `(ends <date>)` with a "Last day: ..." description — so the
calendar shows a deadline, not a smear.

## The page

The result lives at [the events page](events/) — a static page reading a committed
`events.json`, with three views (All / This weekend / Next 30 days), category filter
chips, and a staleness indicator that flags itself if the data hasn't refreshed in a
while. No server, no database, no build step beyond the skill run that produces the
JSON; it's plain HTML/CSS/JS served straight out of the site's `_site` directory.

The current snapshot: 66 events across festivals, music, museums, sports, board games,
trivia, escape rooms, hikes, and markets, plus a handful of Ottawa weekend-trip entries
that got added when the doc's author started tracking those too.

## What broke / surprised

A few things didn't work on the first try:

- **The HEAD-then-GET link check was too narrow.** The advisory link checker
  (`montreal_events/check_links.py`) originally only retried with GET when a HEAD
  request came back `405 Method Not Allowed`. That's the "polite" reason a site rejects
  HEAD, but it turns out sites also reject HEAD as a bot-blocking measure and return
  `403`, which the original logic just recorded as dead. The fix widened the retry to any
  4xx/5xx except a trusted `404` — a one-line change (`response.status_code >= 400 and
  response.status_code != 404` instead of `== 405`), caught in review before it ever
  produced a bad run.

- **The date-window filter hid things that were still running.** In `app.js`,
  `inView()` decides whether an event belongs in the "this weekend" or "this month"
  view. The bug: for a null `start_date` (the common case for an open-ended "until X"
  exhibit), the original code fell back to `end` on both sides of the comparison —
  `(start ?? end) <= win[1]`. That silently collapsed a currently-running exhibit's
  window down to a single day (its end date), so it would vanish from "this weekend" or
  "this month" until that end date arrived, even though it was open the whole time. Also
  caught in review, also a one-line fix: `(start ?? new Date(0)) <= win[1]` treats a
  missing start as "already started," not "starts on the end date."

- **The SEPAQ links look dead, but probably aren't.** All three SEPAQ (Quebec park)
  pages have failed the link checker on every run so far, alongside a few smaller
  sites. All three failing identically, on both HEAD and GET, points more toward
  bot-blocking than actually-dead pages — worth an eyeball in a real browser before
  trusting the checker's "dead" badge on the page.

- **Some categorization is a judgment call, not a fact.** The extraction step has to
  decide a category for things that don't fit neatly — Otakuthon and Cirque du Soleil
  both landed in "other" rather than being forced into festival or music. That's the LLM
  doing its best with an enum that can't cover everything, and it's a fine trade as long
  as it's consistent.

None of these were show-stoppers, but all four are the kind of bug you only find by
running the real pipeline against real data — not something a spec would have caught in
advance.
